issues.original: This is the article in the series where most of the production wins live. The other models are interesting; the LLMs are what every product I have shipped on Bailian has called every minute of every day. The official Qwen API reference is dense and complete; this article is the readable companion that picks one path through it.
issues.fixed: This article in the series covers most of the production wins. The other models are interesting, but the LLMs are what every product I've shipped on Bailian calls every minute of every day. The official Qwen API reference is dense and complete; this article is the readable companion that guides you through it.

issues.original: The Qwen family is large. Most teams overspend by defaulting to [CODE] everywhere. Most teams underspend on quality by defaulting to [CODE]. The right answer is 'match variant to job':
issues.fixed: The Qwen family is large. Most teams overspend by defaulting to [CODE] everywhere and underspend on quality by defaulting to [CODE]. The right approach is to match the variant to the job:

issues.original: Independent of whether you use the OpenAI compat layer or DashScope native, the substance of a chat-completion request is the same: a model id, a messages array, and a parameter block.
issues.fixed: Whether you use the OpenAI compat layer or DashScope native, the core of a chat-completion request remains the same: a model ID, a messages array, and a parameter block.

issues.original: A common mistake is using [CODE] for embedding-style classification. Don't. Use [CODE] with a tight system prompt and you'll cut cost 10× with no quality loss on tasks where you only need a label.
issues.fixed: A common mistake is using [CODE] for embedding-style classification. Instead, use [CODE] with a tight system prompt. This will cut costs by 10× without losing quality on tasks that only require a label.

issues.original: For structured output, do not rely on prompting. Use:
issues.fixed: For structured output, don't rely on prompting. Use:

issues.original: Qwen3 series models support **[CODE]** — it asks the model to produce a reasoning chain before the final answer. Quality goes up, especially on reasoning-heavy tasks. **But you must use streaming.** Non-stream returns a 400.
issues.fixed: Qwen3 series models support **[CODE]**, which prompts the model to produce a reasoning chain before the final answer. This improves quality, especially for reasoning-heavy tasks. **You must use streaming.** Non-streaming requests return a 400 error.

issues.original: I forward [CODE] to my logging system and never to the user. Three reasons: (1) it leaks chain-of-thought IP if customers ever see it, (2) it confuses non-technical readers, (3) it doubles your visible response length.
issues.fixed: I forward [CODE] to my logging system and never to the user for three reasons: (1) it can leak chain-of-thought IP if customers see it, (2) it confuses non-technical readers, and (3) it doubles the visible response length.

issues.original: The single biggest surprise for teams coming from the OpenAI ecosystem: **[CODE] lies about Qwen token counts**. The Qwen tokenizer is not BPE-compatible with [CODE] or [CODE]. If you size your context budget with [CODE], you'll be off by 20-40% on Chinese, 5-10% on English. I lost a Friday night to a RAG pipeline that was 'definitely under the 32k context' by tiktoken count and was actually 41k by Qwen's count.
issues.fixed: The biggest surprise for teams coming from the OpenAI ecosystem is that **[CODE] misrepresents Qwen token counts**. The Qwen tokenizer is not BPE-compatible with [CODE] or [CODE]. If you size your context budget with [CODE], you'll be off by 20-40% for Chinese and 5-10% for English. I once spent a Friday night troubleshooting a RAG pipeline that was 'definitely under the 32k context' according to tiktoken, but was actually 41k by Qwen's count.

issues.original: Of all the Bailian models, Qwen-Omni is the one that has pulled me out of the most product-roadmap holes. "Can you tell me what's happening in this 2-minute promo video?" used to be a 3-week project involving frame extraction, captioning per frame, and a stitch step. With Qwen-Omni it is one HTTP request. But the docs are sparse on the gotchas, and there is one (streaming is mandatory) that has cost more than one team a half-day. Let's not have that be you.
issues.fixed: Of all the Bailian models, Qwen-Omni has saved me from the most product-roadmap issues. "Can you tell me what's happening in this 2-minute promo video?" used to take 3 weeks, involving frame extraction, captioning each frame, and stitching them together. With Qwen-Omni, it's just one HTTP request. However, the documentation lacks details on some pitfalls, like the fact that streaming is mandatory, which has cost more than one team a half-day. Let's avoid that for you.

issues.original: The reason makes sense once you think about it: the model is processing many MB of video and producing a long response. The wire protocol assumes incremental delivery. Holding the whole thing back to send as one blob would block your client for tens of seconds with no progress signal.
issues.fixed: The reason makes sense when you think about it: the model processes large video files and generates a long response. The wire protocol assumes incremental delivery. Sending everything at once would block your client for tens of seconds without any progress signal.

issues.original: If your downstream code expects a complete string, buffer the deltas yourself: It is one extra function. You will write it once.
issues.fixed: If your downstream code expects a complete string, buffer the deltas yourself. It's just one extra function you'll write once.

issues.original: The cost knob the docs are quiet about: [CODE] and [CODE] for images, and the equivalent fps / resize parameters for video. By default Qwen-Omni will process video at native resolution and a default fps. For a 2-minute 1080p clip that is a *lot* of token-equivalents, and the bill scales with it.
issues.fixed: The cost settings the docs don't mention: [CODE] and [CODE] for images, and the equivalent fps/resize parameters for video. By default, Qwen-Omni processes video at native resolution and a default fps. For a 2-minute 1080p clip, that's a *lot* of token-equivalents, and the cost scales accordingly.

issues.original: What I do in production: - **Images for understanding tasks** — [CODE]. Almost no quality loss for "what's in this image" tasks, big cost savings. Set [CODE] so the model never scales up tiny crops. - **Video for description tasks** — pre-resize to 720p before upload, downsample fps to 4 for static-ish content (people talking) or 8 for action content (sports, fast cuts). Above 8 fps you are usually paying for redundant frames. - **Long video** — chunk it. The model has a context limit. For anything over ~3 minutes, split into 90-second chunks, summarize each, then summarize-the-summaries with [CODE]. Same pattern as long-document RAG.
issues.fixed: In production, I do the following: - **Images for understanding tasks** — [CODE]. There's almost no quality loss for "what's in this image" tasks, and it saves a lot of cost. Set [CODE] to prevent the model from scaling up tiny crops. - **Video for description tasks** — pre-resize to 720p before uploading, and downsample fps to 4 for static content (like people talking) or 8 for action content (like sports or fast cuts). Above 8 fps, you're usually paying for redundant frames. - **Long video** — break it into chunks. The model has a context limit. For anything over ~3 minutes, split it into 90-second chunks, summarize each, and then summarize the summaries with [CODE]. This follows the same pattern as long-document RAG.

issues.original: You have two options. The docs cover both. **Path 1 (preferred): upload to OSS, send signed URL.** Then pass [CODE] as the [CODE] field. This is the right answer for anything bigger than 30 seconds because base64 inflates the payload by 33%. **Path 2: base64 inline.** Useful for short clips, avoids the round trip to OSS.
issues.fixed: You have two options, both covered in the docs. **Path 1 (preferred): Upload to OSS and send a signed URL.** Pass [CODE] as the [CODE] field. This is best for anything over 30 seconds because base64 encoding increases the payload by 33%. **Path 2: Inline base64.** This is useful for short clips and avoids the round trip to OSS.

issues.original: The Qwen-Omni audio path looks simple — pass an [CODE] part with [CODE] and [CODE] — but the encoding requirements are strict in ways that don't surface as friendly errors. Get them wrong and you get a 400 with "unsupported audio format" and no further guidance.
issues.fixed: The Qwen-Omni audio path seems simple — pass an [CODE] part with [CODE] and [CODE] — but the encoding requirements are strict and don't provide friendly error messages. If you get them wrong, you'll receive a 400 error with "unsupported audio format" and no further guidance.

issues.original: Wanxiang is the API that has done the most for our marketing pipeline and caused the most production surprises. The model is genuinely good — [CODE] produces 720p clips that pass for an actual video team's output most of the time — but the surface around it is async, native-protocol, has expiring URLs, and rate-limits in non-obvious ways. This article is the version of the docs that has been through six months of 'why is this happening at 2am' tickets.
issues.fixed: Wanxiang is the API that has done the most for our marketing pipeline and caused the most production surprises. The model is genuinely good — [CODE] produces 720p clips that often pass for an actual video team's output — but the surrounding features are async, use a native protocol, have expiring URLs, and rate-limit in non-obvious ways. This article is the version of the docs that has been refined over six months of 'why is this happening at 2am' tickets.

issues.original: There is one flow, repeated for every video: The minimum viable Python:
issues.fixed: The process is the same for every video: The minimum viable Python code is as follows:

issues.original: Polling every second is wasteful and gets you rate-limited. Polling every 30 seconds wastes user time. The backoff schedule I use: Start at 5 seconds, multiply by 1.45 each iteration, cap at 60 seconds. A typical 720p 5-second clip finishes in 30-90 seconds, so the median user waits about 4 polls.
issues.fixed: Polling every second is wasteful and can get you rate-limited. Polling every 30 seconds wastes user time. Here’s the backoff schedule I use: Start at 5 seconds, multiply by 1.45 each iteration, and cap at 60 seconds. A typical 720p 5-second clip finishes in 30-90 seconds, so the median user waits about 4 polls.

issues.original: For a backend service, the right pattern is often *not* to poll inside the request handler. Instead: 1. User submits prompt → you POST to Wanxiang and store [CODE] in your DB. 2. Return immediately with a job URL. 3. A background worker polls and updates the DB when [CODE]. 4. The frontend polls *your* DB, not Wanxiang.
issues.fixed: For a backend service, it's often better not to poll inside the request handler. Instead: 1. User submits a prompt → you POST to Wanxiang and store [CODE] in your DB. 2. Return immediately with a job URL. 3. A background worker polls and updates the DB when [CODE]. 4. The frontend polls your DB, not Wanxiang.

issues.original: The single most expensive mistake I have seen in production: someone fetched the [CODE], displayed it on the site, and then the page broke 24 hours later when the URL stopped resolving. The URLs Wanxiang returns are signed and time-bound. **Always copy the file to your own OSS bucket on success:**
issues.fixed: The most expensive mistake I've seen in production: someone fetched the [CODE], displayed it on the site, and the page broke 24 hours later when the URL expired. The URLs Wanxiang returns are signed and time-bound. **Always copy the file to your own OSS bucket on success:**

issues.original: Same flow, different [CODE] and inputs. I2V takes an [CODE] (OSS-signed URL works); KF2V takes [CODE] and [CODE]. The duration limits are model-dependent (typically 5 or 10 seconds); read the model card before generating.
issues.fixed: The flow is the same, but with different [CODE] and inputs. I2V takes an [CODE] (OSS-signed URL works); KF2V takes [CODE] and [CODE]. The duration limits vary by model (typically 5 or 10 seconds), so check the model card before generating.

issues.original: The most common failure is 'the model generated something, but it ignored half the prompt'. Causes: - Prompt too long. Wanxiang has a soft limit; aggressive trimming helps. - Prompt contradictory ('daytime, dark, neon'). Pick one. - Wrong model variant. T2V will not animate a specific image; you wanted I2V. - Wrong aspect ratio. The [CODE] parameter shapes composition; [CODE] and [CODE] produce different framings.
issues.fixed: The most common failure is when the model generates something but ignores part of the prompt. Causes: - Prompt too long. Wanxiang has a soft limit; aggressive trimming helps. - Contradictory prompt ('daytime, dark, neon'). Pick one. - Wrong model variant. T2V won't animate a specific image; you need I2V. - Wrong aspect ratio. The [CODE] parameter shapes composition; [CODE] and [CODE] produce different framings.

issues.original: Generate three variants per critical prompt with different seeds ([CODE] parameter). One of them is usually the right one.
issues.fixed: Generate three variants per critical prompt with different seeds ([CODE] parameter). One of them is usually correct.

issues.original: Wanxiang is per-second-of-video billed. A 5-second 720p clip is on the order of a few RMB. Concurrent task limits are per-API-key — for production traffic, request a quota increase via the console *before* you launch. The default of (last I checked) 5 concurrent tasks per workspace is fine for prototyping and instantly insufficient for any real product.
issues.fixed: Wanxiang bills per second of video. A 5-second 720p clip costs a few RMB. Concurrent task limits are per API key. For production traffic, request a quota increase via the console before you launch. The default of 5 concurrent tasks per workspace is fine for prototyping but insufficient for a real product.

issues.original: The polling-with-backoff approach in the section above is the simplest pattern that works. For a single user-initiated request it is enough. For a production marketing pipeline that submits 200 videos a day, polling burns API calls and engineering simplicity competes with cost. The alternatives:
issues.fixed: The polling-with-backoff approach in the previous section is the simplest pattern that works. It's sufficient for a single user-initiated request. For a production marketing pipeline that submits 200 videos a day, polling burns API calls, and engineering simplicity competes with cost. The alternatives:

issues.original: Three things webhooks force you to handle: - **Public endpoint required.** DashScope can't POST into your VPC. Either expose via a public load balancer or use a relay (I run an Nginx in front that auth-checks and forwards into the private network). - **Idempotency.** Webhooks can fire twice. Always check whether you've already archived this [CODE] before doing it again. - **Failure mode is silent.** If your webhook endpoint is down when DashScope tries to deliver, you don't get a retry. Always pair the webhook with a 'scan tasks older than 10 minutes that aren't terminal' cleanup job.
issues.fixed: Three things webhooks require: - **Public endpoint.** DashScope can't POST into your VPC. Expose it via a public load balancer or use a relay (I run an Nginx in front that auth-checks and forwards into the private network). - **Idempotency.** Webhooks can fire twice. Always check if you've already archived this [CODE] before doing it again. - **Silent failure.** If your webhook endpoint is down when DashScope tries to deliver, you don't get a retry. Pair the webhook with a 'scan tasks older than 10 minutes that aren't terminal' cleanup job.

issues.original: The reason every Chinese-language product I've worked on ends up calling Qwen-TTS-Flash isn't price — there are cheaper TTS APIs. It's that Qwen-TTS is the only one that handles **mainland Chinese dialects** (Cantonese, Sichuanese, Wu) and English in the same SDK, with voices that don't sound like a 2019 customs announcement.
issues.fixed: Every Chinese-language product I've worked on uses Qwen-TTS-Flash, not because it's the cheapest TTS API, but because it's the only one that handles **mainland Chinese dialects** (Cantonese, Sichuanese, Wu) and English in the same SDK, with voices that don't sound like a 2019 customs announcement.

issues.original: For voice-bot use cases (real-time conversational UIs) you want streaming. The deltas are audio bytes you can write straight to a player or a file:
issues.fixed: For voice-bot use cases (real-time conversational UIs), you want streaming. The deltas are audio bytes you can write directly to a player or a file:

issues.original: Time-to-first-byte on streaming is typically under 400ms in Shanghai region, which is fast enough that a user perceives it as immediate. Non-streaming for a 30-second utterance is closer to 4-6 seconds wall clock — fine for batch narration, sluggish for chat.
issues.fixed: Time-to-first-byte for streaming is typically under 400ms in the Shanghai region, which users perceive as immediate. Non-streaming for a 30-second utterance takes around 4-6 seconds, which is fine for batch narration but sluggish for chat.

issues.original: Qwen-TTS does language *detection* from the text, but if you mix scripts you should set [CODE] explicitly. My production rules:
issues.fixed: Qwen-TTS detects the language from the text, but if you mix scripts, set [CODE] explicitly. My production rules:

issues.original: - Pure Mandarin text → [CODE] (default).
issues.fixed: - Pure Mandarin text: [CODE] (default).

issues.original: - Pure English text → [CODE]. Voices like [CODE] shine here.
issues.fixed: - Pure English text: [CODE]. Voices like [CODE] excel here.

issues.original: - Cantonese text in Traditional Chinese → [CODE], voice [CODE] or [CODE].
issues.fixed: - Cantonese text in Traditional Chinese: [CODE], voice [CODE] or [CODE].

issues.original: - Mixed CJK + English (the common case for tech narration) → leave language unset, the model handles code-switch surprisingly well.
issues.fixed: - Mixed CJK + English (common in tech narration): leave the language unset; the model handles code-switching surprisingly well.

issues.original: > **Tip:** For dialect work, **always** A/B against native speakers before launch. Qwen-TTS Cantonese is good but not perfect on tones — a one-syllable tone error in Cantonese can change the meaning entirely.
issues.fixed: > **Tip:** For dialect work, **always** A/B test against native speakers before launch. Qwen-TTS Cantonese is good but not perfect on tones—a one-syllable tone error can completely change the meaning.

issues.original: Marketing scripts are long. The pattern for a 60-second voiceover:
issues.fixed: Marketing scripts are long. Here’s the pattern for a 60-second voiceover:

issues.original: Why split? Two reasons: (1) per-call latency is much lower for short utterances, so synthesizing in parallel is faster; (2) you can patch a single bad sentence by re-rolling just that one without redoing the whole take. We use this for ad spots — the [CODE] tags handle the inter-sentence pauses, and the parallel synth means a 60-second clip is ready in ~4 seconds.
issues.fixed: Why split? Two reasons: (1) per-call latency is lower for short utterances, making parallel synthesis faster; (2) you can fix a single bad sentence without redoing the whole take. We use this for ad spots—the [CODE] tags handle inter-sentence pauses, and parallel synthesis means a 60-second clip is ready in ~4 seconds.

issues.original: Per-second-of-output-audio billing. Streaming and non-streaming bill identically. A 60-second ad spot is in the few-RMB range — much cheaper than the cost of a voice actor's hourly rate, and fast enough for the marketing team to iterate dozens of variations in an afternoon.
issues.fixed: Billing is per second of output audio. Streaming and non-streaming costs are the same. A 60-second ad spot costs a few RMB—much cheaper than a voice actor's hourly rate and fast enough for the marketing team to iterate dozens of variations in an afternoon.

issues.original: Bailian gives you three layers of voice control, and which one you reach for matters more than people think. The order of cost, complexity, and control is opposite to the order of 'fancy':
issues.fixed: Bailian offers three layers of voice control, and which one you choose matters more than people think. The order of cost, complexity, and control is the opposite of 'fancy':

issues.original: **Preset voices.** The 40+ voices in the catalog. Use these unless you have a specific reason not to. They cover Mandarin male/female across all common ages and registers, plus Cantonese, Sichuanese, Wu, English (US/UK), and a handful of 'character' voices for narration work. Per-second cost is the standard rate; latency is the lowest of the three; quality is studio-grade. About 90% of my production traffic is preset-voice.
issues.fixed: **Preset voices.** The 40+ voices in the catalog. Use these unless you have a specific reason not to. They cover Mandarin male/female across all common ages and registers, plus Cantonese, Sichuanese, Wu, English (US/UK), and a few 'character' voices for narration. Per-second cost is the standard rate; latency is the lowest; quality is studio-grade. About 90% of my production traffic uses preset voices.

issues.fixed: - You have proper nouns or technical terms the model mispronounces (especially in CJK + English code-switch contexts: 'DSL' often comes out as 'D-S-L' instead of 'deh-es-el').

issues.original: **Voice cloning (timbre transfer).** Bailian exposes a separate clone API where you upload 10-30 seconds of reference audio and get back a voice id you can use in subsequent calls. This is real but hedge it with two warnings:
issues.fixed: **Voice cloning (timbre transfer).** Bailian provides a separate clone API where you upload 10-30 seconds of reference audio and get a voice ID for subsequent calls. This is real, but consider two warnings:

issues.original: - **Quality varies.** Clones of clear, single-speaker, studio-recorded reference audio are excellent. Clones of phone-quality, noisy, or multi-speaker reference audio are uncanny in bad ways. Always hand-screen clones before production use.
issues.fixed: - **Quality varies.** Clones of clear, single-speaker, studio-recorded audio are excellent. Clones of phone-quality, noisy, or multi-speaker audio are uncanny. Always hand-screen clones before production use.

issues.original: - **Legal/ethical surface.** Cloning a real human's voice without consent is increasingly illegal in many jurisdictions and is explicitly banned in Bailian's ToS. Clone only voices you own o
issues.fixed: - **Legal/ethical concerns.** Cloning a real human's voice without consent is increasingly illegal in many jurisdictions and is explicitly banned in Bailian's ToS. Clone only voices you own.
